clinicalPresentation.chiefComplaint: Abdominal pain for 12 hours, started around the navel, now shifted to right lower abdomen

HOPI.onset: Sudden onset periumbilical colicky pain ~12 hours ago
HOPI.progression: Pain has migrated and localised to RLQ, now constant and sharp

effects.penaltyMsg: Disease progressed to localised peritonitis — earlier intervention would have been safer.
Appendix has perforated! Peritonitis developing. Open surgery required immediately.
Patient died from septic shock secondary to faecal peritonitis.

results.early: WBC 12,400/μL (mild leukocytosis). Neutrophils 78%. Hb 14.2 g/dL. Platelets normal.
results.localized: WBC 18,200/μL (significant leukocytosis). Neutrophils 85%, left shift. Bands 8%.

interpretation.early: Mild leukocytosis supports an acute inflammatory process. Consistent with appendicitis.
interpretation.localized: Significant leukocytosis with left shift — active bacterial infection spreading.
interpretation.perforation: Severe leukocytosis with band forms — meets sepsis criteria.

results.early: Clear urine. No RBCs, no WBCs, no bacteria. Protein absent. Glucose absent.
results.localized: Trace WBCs (sterile pyuria from adjacent inflammation). No bacteria on culture.
results.perforation: Concentrated urine. Trace WBCs. No organisms. Reflects dehydration in sepsis.

interpretation.early: Normal urine effectively rules out UTI and renal colic — important differentials.
interpretation.localized: Sterile pyuria from adjacent peritoneal irritation, not UTI.
interpretation.perforation: Oliguria reflects poor renal perfusion in septic shock.

results.early: CRP: 22 mg/L (mildly elevated, NL <10). Early acute-phase response.
results.localized: CRP: 94 mg/L (significantly elevated). Active inflammatory process.
results.perforation: CRP: 287 mg/L (markedly elevated). Consistent with peritonitis/sepsis.

interpretation.early: Mild CRP elevation is expected in early appendicitis.
interpretation.localized: High CRP supports spreading peritoneal inflammation.
interpretation.perforation: Markedly raised CRP is a marker of severe sepsis.

results.early: Appendix visualised: 9mm diameter, non-compressible. Mild periappendiceal fat stranding. Consistent with early appendicitis.
results.localized: Appendix 12mm, non-compressible with appendicolith. Periappendiceal fluid collection 5mm. Localised inflammation.
results.perforation: Perforated appendix — complex heterogeneous collection in RIF. Free fluid in pelvis.

interpretation.early: Diameter >6mm + non-compressible is sonographically diagnostic of appendicitis.
interpretation.localized: Appendicolith with surrounding collection — localised peritonitis confirmed.
interpretation.perforation: Perforation confirmed. CT needed to map extent of contamination.

tests.name: CT Abdomen
tests.fullName: CT Abdomen & Pelvis with IV Contrast

results.early: Appendix 9mm. Mild periappendiceal stranding. No perforation. No free gas. Uncomplicated appendicitis.
results.localized: Appendix 13mm with appendicolith. Periappendiceal fluid collection 3×2cm. Localised peritonitis. No free gas.
results.perforation: Perforated appendix. Extraluminal free air. Pelvic abscess 4×5cm. Diffuse peritoneal contamination.

interpretation.early: Gold standard imaging confirms uncomplicated appendicitis.
interpretation.perforation: Free air + pelvic collection — open laparotomy and washout required.

results.early: Bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL (NL). AST 28 (NL). ALT 22 (NL). ALP 88 (NL). All within normal limits.
results.localized: LFT: All parameters within normal limits.
results.perforation: ALP mildly elevated (non-specific in sepsis). Other parameters normal.

interpretation.early: Normal LFT — hepatic/biliary pathology unlikely. Not useful in this context.
interpretation.perforation: Mild non-specific changes from systemic sepsis.

results.early: No gas under diaphragm. No bowel obstruction. Rightward scoliosis (pain splinting). Non-diagnostic.
results.localized: Sentinel loop in RLQ. Slightly dilated loops — localised ileus. No free gas.
results.perforation: Free gas under right dome of diaphragm — PNEUMOPERITONEUM. Confirms perforation.

interpretation.early: Normal X-ray does not exclude appendicitis. Poor sensitivity in early disease.
interpretation.localized: Sentinel loop suggests localised peritoneal irritation.
interpretation.perforation: Free gas is a surgical emergency — immediate intervention required.

results.early: Sinus tachycardia 94 bpm. No ST changes. Normal QRS. Normal axis.
results.localized: Sinus tachycardia 106 bpm. No ischaemic changes. Normal.
results.perforation: Sinus tachycardia 128 bpm. No specific changes.

interpretation.early: Tachycardia consistent with pain/anxiety. ECG not indicated for this presentation.
interpretation.localized: Tachycardia from sepsis. No primary cardiac pathology.
interpretation.perforation: Septic tachycardia. ECG not useful here.

perforation.note: Aggressive fluid resuscitation for septic shock.

general.name: Keep NPO
general.fullName: Nil Per Oral + NG Tube Insertion

early.note: Correct pre-op preparation. Patient fasted for safe anaesthesia.

localized.note: NPO maintained. NG tube to decompress distended abdomen.

perforation.note: NPO + NG decompression essential in peritonitis.

early.note: Pain relief administered. Humane care — does not mask diagnosis at this stage.

localized.note: IV analgesia given. Important for patient comfort.

perforation.note: Morphine 5mg IV given. Part of sepsis management bundle.

localized.note: Hourly vitals and urine output — important in peritonitis.

early.note: No benefit in appendicitis. Oral route contraindicated in NPO patient.

localized.note: Inappropriate — patient is NPO. Oral medications contraindicated.

perforation.note: Wrong and harmful — oral medications in perforated viscus.

early.note: Antispasmodics NOT indicated for appendicitis. May mask clinical signs.

localized.note: Dangerous — masks worsening peritoneal signs. Significantly delays surgery.

perforation.note: Critically harmful — masks signs in deteriorating patient.

early.note: Prophylactic antibiotics before surgery. Slows bacterial progression.

localized.note: Broad spectrum antibiotics essential before surgery.

perforation.note: IV antibiotics for bacteraemia. Necessary but not sufficient — surgery is mandatory.

early.note: Excellent! Laparoscopic appendectomy performed successfully. Minimal blood loss. Patient recovering well.

localized.note: Laparoscopic appendectomy completed. Peritoneal washout performed. Patient recovering.

perforation.note: Laparoscopic approach not safe after perforation with peritonitis. Use open laparotomy instead.

early.note: Open appendectomy successful. Slightly more invasive than needed but good outcome.

localized.note: Open appendectomy with thorough washout performed. Patient recovering.

perforation.note: Emergency open laparotomy performed. Appendix removed, abdomen washed out, drain placed. Patient in ICU — recovering.

appendicitis.name: Antibiotics Only (Conservative)
appendicitis.fullName: Conservative Management — Antibiotics Only, No Surgery

early.note: Suboptimal — conservative management has 25% failure rate. Surgery is the standard of care.

localized.note: Wrong — conservative management is contraindicated in localised peritonitis.

perforation.note: Catastrophic error — antibiotics alone in perforated appendicitis leads to death.

early.note: BLUNDER — active observation without treatment in confirmed appendicitis directly accelerates perforation.

localized.note: BLUNDER — waiting with established peritonitis. Patient deteriorating rapidly.

perforation.note: FATAL BLUNDER — no surgery in perforated appendicitis.

endMessages.perfect: Outstanding! Rapid diagnosis with minimal cost and zero complications. Theatre-ready decision making.
endMessages.good: Good work. Patient recovered well. Minor inefficiencies in test ordering or timing — review your approach.
endMessages.average: Patient survived but management had errors. Delayed diagnosis or excess investigations slowed you down.
endMessages.poor: Patient suffered serious complications from delayed or incorrect management. Review appendicitis guidelines thoroughly.
